coredata.dc:title: Femtosecond laser synthesis of nitrogen-doped luminescent carbon dots from acetonitrile
coredata.dc:creator: Astafiev, Artyom A.
Shakhov, Aleksander M.
Kritchenkov, Andreii S.
Khrustalev, Victor N.
Shepel, Denis V.
Nadtochenko, Victor A.
Tskhovrebov, Alexander G.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel promising class of nanomaterials with unique properties. Most of synthetic methods to obtain CDs employ hydrothermal synthesis, breaking down larger carbon materials by laser irradiation, arc discharge, ultrasonication or electrolysis. Here we describe a conceptually new bottom-up approach to nitrogen-rich carbonaceous nanoparticles via high intensity femtosecond laser irradiation of neat acetonitrile where the latter serves a source of the doping N atoms. Selected photophysical properties of the new material and mechanism of the photoluminescence (PL) are discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Femtochemistry
Physical chemistry
Carbon nanomaterials
Fluorescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the RFBR , Grants No. 18-03-01121 and 19-53-52007 . This work was supported by the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (award no. 075-03-2020-223 (FSSF-2020-0017)) and the RUDN University Strategic Academic Leadership Program. Experiments were performed using equipment of Semenov FRCCP CCE (no.506694).

Experiments were performed using equipment of Semenov FRCCP CCE (no.506694).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Neat acetonitrile after 10 min, 1 h and 6 h of femtosecond laser irradiation. b) AFM image of CDs. Color scale corresponds to local height of the sample surface. Scale bar is 1 μm. c) TEM image of CDs on a copper grid. d) Diameter distribution of nanoparticles based on the statistical analysis of the TEM image. e,f) HRTEM images of acetonitrile-derived CDs.
a) XRD pattern, b) Raman spectrum (D-band at 1310 cm−1, G band at 1590 cm−1, and 2D band at 2650 cm−1). c) FTIR spectrum of acetonitrile-derived CDs.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum of CDs. High resolution XPS spectra of O1s (b), N1s (c) and C1s (d) and their decomposition into spectral components.

a) UV-VIS absorption spectra of laser irradiation products as a function of irradiation time. The spectrum of isolated CDs is plotted on the same graph for comparison. b) PL spectra of isolated CDs in ethanol at different excitation wavelengths. c) PLE spectra of isolated CDs in ethanol recorded at 380 and 430 nm. PLE spectra have a common peak at 250 nm and separate peaks at ~330 and 350 nm.

a) Luminescence decay kinetics (430 nm) of CDs produced by 15 min and 10 h laser irradiation of MeCN and dissolved in EtOH and their fit with a two exponential decay function. b) Amplitude-weighted average luminescence lifetime (τ) of CDs (430 nm) in EtOH and quantum yield (Φ) of emission excited at 355 nm vs irradiation time. c) Average luminescence lifetime of CDs produced by 15 min and 10 h laser irradiation in EtOH vs emission wavelength. d) Normalized integral emission intensity of CDs in EtOH under continuous irradiation at 355 nm vs time.

head.ce:title: Femtosecond laser synthesis of nitrogen-doped luminescent carbon dots from acetonitrile

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: N.N. Semenov Federal Research Center for Chemical Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel promising class of nanomaterials with unique properties. Most of synthetic methods to obtain CDs employ hydrothermal synthesis, breaking down larger carbon materials by laser irradiation, arc discharge, ultrasonication or electrolysis. Here we describe a conceptually new bottom-up approach to nitrogen-rich carbonaceous nanoparticles via high intensity femtosecond laser irradiation of neat acetonitrile where the latter serves a source of the doping N atoms. Selected photophysical properties of the new material and mechanism of the photoluminescence (PL) are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots is an emerging class of nanomaterials which hold promise in the fields of bioimaging, photocatalysis, sensing, as theranostic agents, components of light emitting diodes etc. [1–10] Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles are currently regarded as competitors to more common semiconductor quantum dots due to their enhanced photostability, facile postsynthetic modification, low toxicity, environmental friendliness and low fabrication cost [2,11]. Importantly, optical properties of CDs are highly tunable and can be adjusted by changing their size, morphology, dopants and surface modification [12–16]. As a consequence, a lot of effort was invested in their synthesis and chemical modification which resulted in a plethora of synthetic methods which, in turn, could be formally split into two general approaches: top-down and bottom-up synthesis [3,5]. While the former is based on breaking down larger carbon materials by laser irradiation, arc discharge, ultrasonication or electrolysis, the latter one involves solution-based thermolysis of organic precursors [17] and often turns to be a method of choice for the synthesis of CDs. However, fine control of particle size, crystallinity, morphology and chemical composition of CDs still remains a challenge [15], motivating the research towards the development of conceptually new approaches to the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials. Moreover, fabrication of CD-based optical devices like LEDs or photonic crystals requires ability to precisely arrange small quantities of CDs into microscale patterns. Thus far, only a few exotic methods for microscale space-selective synthesis have been demonstrated [18]. Direct femtosecond laser writing is a potentially powerful technique for manufacturing of 2D or even 3D micropatterns of CDs with high accuracy and spatial resolution similar to laser writing of semiconductor quantum dots [19]. Our technique can take advantage of effective nonlinear absorption in strong optical fields of tightly focused laser pulses to carry out synthesis of CDs in a microscale reaction volume as small as hundreds of nanometers.

Another feature of femtosecond pulses is an ability to produce strong photoionization even in optically transparent materials and, thus, allow reaction pathways which are unavailable for thermally activated reactions and produce CDs with novel properties from novel precursors. Despite potential advantages of direct laser writing, only a very few reports of pulsed laser bottom-up synthesis of luminescent CDs from organic molecules have been published [20,21], and in all cases only arenes were employed as precursors. Potential of laser pulses to generate CDs from non-aromatic molecules remains completely unexplored.

Here we describe an efficient synthesis of CDs under intensive femtosecond laser irradiation of acetonitrile which is a cheap and readily available starting material for the production of N-doped CDs. Nitrogen doping is a common way for enhancing optical or catalytic properties of CDs [15,22]. Finally, acetonitrile is a stable material, which graphitizes only under extreme conditions [23], and to the best of our knowledge, production of CDs from acetonitrile by other methods has never been reported. The results below demonstrate that femtosecond laser-induced approach has untapped potential in bottom-up synthesis of (heteroatom-doped) carbon nanomaterials.

Synthesis of nanoparticles. Acetonitrile (2 mL) was placed in a scintillation vial and irradiated with femtosecond laser pulses at 800 nm wavelength amplified by regenerative amplifier (Spitfire, Spectra-Physics) and focused by a spherical lens with numerical aperture of NA = 0.3. The pulse repetition rate, duration and energy were 1 kHz, 50 fs and 1.4 mJ, respectively. Based on the estimate of the laser beam at the waist the fluence is 1.7 × 104 J/cm2 and the power density is 1.7·1017 W/cm2. Irradiation was carried out at room temperature. The pale-yellow precipitate formed upon irradiation was isolated by filtration, washed with MeCN (3 mL), Et2O (3 × 3 mL) and dried under vacuum.

Atomic-force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). Solid CDs (5 mg) were dissolved in 200 μL of UV-grade ethanol (Acros Organics). For AFM analysis the solution was further diluted with ethanol (1:20). A drop of this solution was placed on a borosilicate glass coverslip, which was pretreated with UV-light from a quartz mercury lamp for 10 min to clean the surface. The sample was scanned with an AFM unit (SMENA-B, NT-MDT) in an intermittent contact mode. TEM measurements employed a target-oriented approach for the optimization of the analytic measurements [24]. A drop of ethanol solution was placed on a 3 mm copper grid with lacey carbon film and fixed in a grid holder. Samples morphology was studied using Hitachi transmission electron microscope. Images were acquired in bright-field TEM mode at 100 kV accelerating voltage.
C, H, and N elemental analyses. The analyses were carried out on a Perkin Elmer 2400 CHN analyzer.
Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. FTIR spectra were recorded using Bruker LUMOS II FTIR microscope-spectrometer (courtesy of Bruker Ltd. Moscow).

Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectra were recorded using a custom-made Raman spectrometer setup. The sample was irradiated with a continuous wave laser beam from a Ti:Sapphire laser (Tsunami, Spectra-Physics) at 683 nm wavelength and 100 mW power. The scattered light was filtered with a 700 nm long-pass dielectric filter (FELH0700, Thorlabs) and coupled to a monochromator/spectrometer (SP300i, Acton) with a 150 G/mm grating. Raman spectra were recorded by an ICCD-camera (PI-MAX, Roper Scientific).

Optical spectroscopy. Irradiated acetonitrile was diluted with ethanol to a 1:3000 vol ratio. Absorption spectra were measured in a 4 mL quartz cuvette using a UV-VIS spectrometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu) and luminescence emission and excitation spectra in the same cuvette - using a spectrofluorimeter (RF-5031PC, Shimadzu). Absorption and luminescence spectra of irradiated acetonitrile samples in other solvents as well as absorption and luminescence spectra of filtered precipitate were measured in a similar way using an arbitrary concentration. The luminescence quantum yield was determined using the slope method relative to the reference fluorophore, which was the ethanol solution of anthracene (Φ = 27%) excited at 356 nm, using a series of ethanol solutions of the sample with varying concentrations. The quantum yield was calculated by the formula:
Φ x
=
Φ s
(
G x
G s
)
where Φ is the quantum yield, G is the slope of the dependence of the integral luminescence intensity on the value of absorption in solution, and the subscripts x and s refer to the sample and reference, respectively.

The luminescence lifetime was measured using frequency-doubled pulses of a femtosecond titanium–sapphire oscillator (Tsunami, Spectra-Physics) with a central wavelength of 355 nm, repetition rate of 80 MHz, duration of 100 fs, and pulse energy of 10 pJ. After passing through a FESH0750 dielectric filter (Thorlabs) mounted at an angle of 45°, femtosecond laser pulses were coupled into an objective lens (Olympus, 20 × , 0.55 NA) and focused into a penicillin vial with a sample. Luminescence was collected by the same lens and directed to a monochromator (Acton SP300i), where it was detected by the photomultiplier tube of a time-correlated photon counting system (SPC-730, Becker&Hickl GmbH), which recorded the luminescence decay kinetics in the time range of 0–12.5 ns with a resolution of 150 ps. Luminescence decay kinetics were fit with a biexponential decay function I(t) = A1exp(-t/T1)+ A2exp(-t/T2) using Origin 9 software. Coefficients A1 and A2 were normalized so that A1+A2 = 1. Amplitude-weighted lifetime was calculated using parameters of the biexponential fit as τ = A1T1+A2T2. To register the luminescence anisotropy kinetics, luminescence decay kinetics at 440 nm emission wavelength for the parallel and perpendicular polarizations relative to the polarization of the exciting radiation were measured using an additional linear polarizer placed in front of the monochromator entrance. The fluorescence anisotropy was calculated by the formula
R(t)=(I‖-α(λ)I⊥)/(I‖+2α(λ)I⊥)
where α is the empirically determined correction factor and I|| and I⊥ are the intensities of the luminescence with parallel and perpendicular polarizations, respectively. The time during which the anisotropy decreases by a factor of e in comparison with the maximum was taken as the mean rotational diffusion time τrot. The average hydrodynamic volume of particles was calculated by the formula
V =
k B
T
τ
r o t
η
where k B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature, and η is the dynamic viscosity of the solution.
NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance III; chemical shifts (δ) are given in ppm relative to TMS.
X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were performed on a Drone-7 X-ray diffractometer.

The lattice spacing was calculated from the diffraction maximum 2θmax using the Bragg's law:
λ = 2dsin(θ)
where λ is the copper wavelength equal to 0.15406 nm.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source.

Irradiation of neat acetonitrile with 50 fs laser pulses at 800 nm with a pulse repetition rate of 1 kHz resulted in a gradual colour change to yellow already after 10 min and then to dark-brown after 6 h of permanent irradiation (Fig. 1 a). The observed darkening of the reaction mixture was accompanied by the formation of a significant amount of pale-yellow precipitate, which was isolated by filtration and dried under vacuum.

Characterization of the solid by the atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has revealed that it consists of nanosized particles (Fig. 1b and c). The diameter of most the particles felt in the interval between 2 and 6 nm with a median diameter of ca. 3.7 nm (Fig. 1d), which is much smaller than those of the counterparts prepared hydrothermally [25].

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) demonstrated the presence of nanoparticles with two distinctive structures. Smaller particles (ca. 2–3 nm) consisted of several parallel layers (Fig. 1f), whereas an oblong onion-like structures with concentric layers were typical for particles of larger diameters (Fig. 1e). In both cases the interlayer distance was unusually large (0.7–0.75 nm). In many other cases the structure of nanoparticles couldn't be clearly resolved, probably due to their highly disordered character or small size of crystalline domains.
C, H, N elemental analyses revealed that CDs had an empirical formula of C1 · 75H2 · 16N1O0.32 versus C2H3N1 in acetonitrile, which demonstrated that the new material is enriched in nitrogen.

The O atoms which are incorporated into the material during the synthesis could derive from air dioxygen and/or water. Importantly, the powders obtained in the course of 8 or 24 h of irradiation gave very similar C, H, N elemental analyses (Fig. S1). It indicates that the same material could be generated regardless the duration of the synthesis and that the novel synthetic procedure is reliable and reproducible.

XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a) were well reproducible for samples obtained at different irradiation times and exhibited a diffraction peak with a maximum at 2θ = 20.5°, what confirmed a crystallinity of nanoparticles. The peak at 20.5° was broadened due to a small size of crystalline particles, and its maximum corresponded to the lattice period of ca. 0.43 nm, which is spacing between graphenic layers in graphite-like crystalline domains of nanoparticles. It was larger than the spacing in graphite (~0.34 nm), probably due to the presence of functional groups within the lattices. Importantly, XRD patterns showed a weak peak at ca. 10–14° (Fig. 2a), which suggested a presence of a faction with a larger lattice period (~0.65–0.9 nm). This minor fraction was also observed by HRTEM.

In order to get more insights about the nature of N-doped CDs we recorded their Raman spectrum which exhibited characteristic D-band at 1315 cm−1 and G-band at 1580 cm−1 (overlapping with defect D′ band at 1630 cm−1) as well as weaker combination bands 2D and D + D’ (Fig. 2b, S3, Table S1) [26]. Thus, the new material contained a significant amount of sp
2 C atoms and graphite-like domains comprising hexagonal aromatic rings. High D to G intensity ratio (ca. 2.7:1) indicated a high level of disorder in the structure what might have been caused by high number of the N atoms, as demonstrated by the elemental analyses, and their random distribution within the lattice [27]. Detailed analysis of Raman spectrum showed the intensity ratio between D and D’ bands intensities equal to approximately 12:1. Such ratio is indicative of predominately sp
3 -type defects [28]. Overall, Raman spectrum of MeCN-derived CDs was similar to the previously synthesized N-doped CDs or N-graphenes, but contained a more intense D peak [29].

Further, we recorded Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra for solid N-doped CDs. Importantly, the results were totally reproducible and did not depend on the duration of the irradiation. In addition, the spectra exhibited only a very weak CN vibration bands at ca. 2200 cm−1, which indicated that the reaction proceeded via addition to the triple CN bond [30–33]. The spectra featured a very broad band at ca. 3200 cm−1 (Fig. 2c) which could be attributed to –NH2 and/or –OH groups, adsorbed water, and another broad and intense band in the range of 1000–1800 cm−1 which is characteristic to the N-containing heterocycles in sp
2
-hybridized aromatic domains of CDs [34,35]. Presence of this band confirms incorporation of nitrogen atoms into sp
2 -hybridized carbon domains. Interestingly, only weak Raman bands corresponding to methyl and methylene groups were detected, which indicates on a small content of alkyl chains. Large amount of amino and hydroxy groups provided a good solubility of the new material in common organic solvents and allowed us to perform liquid state NMR analysis. 1H and 13C NMR spectra (DMSO‑d 6) contain sharp and broad peaks in the aromatic and aliphatic regions which additionally support the presence of aromatic domains, sp
3 C atoms and terminal H atoms attached to such C atoms (Fig. S2).

The structure of CDs was further studied by the XPS. The spectrum (Fig. 3 a) exhibited three sharp peaks attributed to O, N and C atoms. The latter three peaks were decomposed into several components (Fig. 3b–d).

Oxygen peak components corresponded to CO in quinone and amide groups (O1), carboxylic (O2) and phenolic OH (O3) groups [36]. N1 peak was attributed to pyridinic N atoms, while N2 and N3 peaks corresponded to amine groups and pyrrolic N atoms, respectively [37]. XPS results were in accord with the FTIR data. The most intense peak (C1) in the carbon spectrum corresponded to the C atoms attached to other carbons and/or hydrogens, while three smaller peaks (C2–C4) corresponded to heteroatom-bonded carbons. C1 peak at 350 eV was shifted to higher energies compared to what was observed for earlier described graphitic nanomaterials (344–344.5 eV) [38] which could be attributed to extremely high nitrogen doping and a large sp
3 carbon content.

Overall, combination of spectroscopic data, XRD and HRTEM imaging indicated that acetonitrile-derived CDs consisted of nitrogen-doped and strongly disordered graphenic layers decorated with surface functional groups. These layers stack parallel to each other (similar to graphene nanodots) or form concentric structures similar to carbon onions [39]. Lattice period of acetonitrile-derived CDs (0.43 nm and even up to 0.7–0.75 nm for some nanoparticles) was considerably larger than d002 spacing of graphite lattice (~0.34 nm) typical for crystalline domains in CDs [40].

Some researchers explained unusual lattice spacing in carbon dots by formation of ordered polymeric structures of sp
3 -hybridized carbon. High degree of graphitization, revealed by Raman analysis, and small content of alkyl chains, detected by FTIR spectroscopy, rules out such a possibility in our case. A large lattice spacing could be explained by presence of functional groups on surfaces and edges of graphenic layers, which increase interlayer distance [41].

The UV–Vis spectroscopy showed that the product of acetonitrile irradiation had optical absorption mainly in the UV spectral range with a tail in the visible region (Fig. 3a). Monitoring of the reaction mixtures’ absorption as a function of the irradiation time revealed the emergence of the peak at 250–260 nm which upon further irradiation smoothed out while a broad absorption shoulder gradually appeared at ca. 230 nm. This can indicate an initial formation of the intermediate which further converted into CDs. This conclusion is supported by the fact that CDs which were isolated as a precipitate had the same absorption shoulder at 230 nm. Spectral integrals were proportional to the irradiation time which suggested zero-order kinetics of the process (Fig. S4). Position of both absorption bands corresponds to π→π* absorption in aromatic domains of graphene and graphene oxide [42], whereas second derivative analysis revealed presence of additional weak bands at 305 and 325 nm attributable to n→π* absorptions in CO or other O- and N-containing functional groups [43] (Fig. S5).

When a solution of CDs was subjected to UV light, it showed a strong fluorescence seen to the naked eye. PL spectra of CDs in ethanol exhibited a remarkable feature: they showed two emission peaks. Peak 1 had an emission maximum at 380 nm and excitation maximum at 320–330 nm, while peak 2 had an emission maximum at 430 nm and excitation maximum at 340–350 nm (Fig. 4 b and c).

Interestingly, both peaks were excitation-dependent: their emission maxima were monotonously red-shifted when excitation wavelength was increased (Fig. 4b). Such excitation dependent emission violated Vavilov-Kasha rule, which is typical for CDs and explained by dots’ size heterogeneity [13] or variations of surface groups composition [44]. Another remarkable peculiarity of the new CDs was their large Stokes shift (ca. 0.7 eV for peak 2).

Two separate emission peaks can be attributed to several different luminescent domains in an overall structure of CDs. In order to get some insights about the structure of these luminescent structural motifs we studied a solvent influence on the emission of CDs. Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of both luminescence peaks experienced a blue shift of their maxima with an increase of solvent polarity (Fig. S6), which indicated an existence of n→π* transitions within the chemical groups with lone pairs. These lone pairs arguably belong to the N atoms of either amine groups or pyridinic moieties.

Remarkably, both emission peaks shared a common excitation band at ~250 nm (Fig. 4c) and had their separate excitation bands in >300 nm wavelength region. The band at ~250 nm probably corresponds to π→π* transition in CDs’ aromatic domains. Moreover, presence of this band indicates that excitation energy can be transferred from aromatic domains to emissive surface states [45,46].

Further, we analyzed characteristics of the practically important visible emission peak 2 as a function of irradiation time. Luminescence decay kinetics of the peak 2 excited at 355 nm and monitored at the emission maximum (430 nm) was nonexponential, but could be fit with a biexponential decay (Fig. 5 a). Both fast and slow decay times as well as the ratio between the slow and fast decay components, increased with increase of irradiation time (Fig. S7). Consequently, in the course of laser irradiation luminescence decay kinetics became slower as illustrated by amplitude-weighted average emission lifetime (Fig. 5b). Luminescence quantum yield measured at the same excitation wavelength also increased during the first 2 h of laser irradiation and then reached almost a constant value of ~5.5%. The increase of the quantum yield might be related to changes in CDs size [13]. Time-dependent anisotropy kinetics experiments in solution revealed that CDs diameters are of nanometer scale, what is consistent with AFM and TEM results, and they gradually grow in the course of laser irradiation (Fig. S8).

Time-resolved luminescence measurements showed a dependence of luminescence decay on emission wavelength (Fig. 5c). Wavelength-dependent luminescence decay is typical for carbon dots [47,48]. It indicates that the visible luminescence is inhomogeneous and corresponds to an ensemble of emissive transitions with different spectral positions and decay rates.

Importantly, novel CDs exhibited high photostability. When a solution of acetonitrile-derived CDs in EtOH was subjected to a continuous irradiation at 355 nm, the emission intensity decreased slightly during the first 20 min but showed almost no change further (Fig. 5d). High photostability makes laser-synthesized CDs attractive for photoluminescence applications.

In conclusion, we have developed a novel simple method for the production of N-doped CDs from acetonitrile as a source of the N atoms, which is a promising approach to the synthesis of (heteroatom doped) CDs. Importantly, the method is well-reproducible and new CDs exhibit unique physical characteristics distinctive from those of obtained via traditional approaches. Principal characteristics of acetonitrile-derived CDs, such as positions of emission peaks, luminescence quantum yield and mean particle sizes, were remarkably stable and exhibited little dependence on irradiation time. Further studies into synthesis from various precursors, structures, properties and applications of laser-generated CDs from our laboratory are underway and will be reported in due course.

Artyom A. Astafiev: Writing - original draftWriting – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing. Aleksander M. Shakhov: Investigation, Validation, Methodology. Andreii S. Kritchenkov: Investigation, Methodology. Victor N. Khrustalev: Investigation, Methodology. Denis V. Shepel: Investigation. Victor A. Nadtochenko: Conceptualization, Supervision. Alexander G. Tskhovrebov: Writing - original draftWriting – original draft, Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the RFBR, Grants No. 18-03-01121 and 19-53-52007. This work was supported by the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (award no. 075-03-2020-223 (FSSF-2020-0017)) and the RUDN University Strategic Academic Leadership Program. Experiments were performed using equipment of Semenov FRCCP CCE (no.506694).
